steps.title: Identify the grammatical rule
steps.content:
The sentence opens with "By reducing the runoff flowing into city sewers," — a participial phrase. The noun immediately following the comma must be the thing performing that action, or the modifier dangles.
  • Choice A — the mitigation of both street flooding and the resulting pollution of nearby waterways has been achieved by bioswales.: Puts "the mitigation" right after the comma; mitigation does not reduce runoff — the bioswales do. Dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice B — the bioswales have mitigated both street flooding and the resulting pollution of nearby waterways.: Places "the bioswales" immediately after the comma; the bioswales are exactly what reduces runoff, so the modifier attaches correctly. ✓
  • Choice C — the bioswales' mitigation of both street flooding and the resulting pollution of nearby waterways has been achieved.: Puts "the bioswales' mitigation" after the comma; the possessive noun phrase makes mitigation the subject, not the bioswales themselves. Dangling modifier. ✗
  • Choice D — both street flooding and the resulting pollution of nearby waterways have been mitigated by bioswales.: Puts "street flooding and pollution" right after the comma, implying that flooding and pollution reduce runoff — logically impossible. Dangling modifier. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the subject match
steps.content: Only B makes "the bioswales" the grammatical subject immediately after the modifying phrase, correctly identifying what performs the reduction. That matches Choice B — the bioswales have mitigated both street flooding and the resulting pollution of nearby waterways..
